title: Blueberry custard tart
subtitle: Shortcrust tart with vanilla custard and fresh blueberries
excerpt: A blueberry custard tart: a crisp, sweet shortcrust case filled with a thick vanilla pastry cream, topped with fresh blueberries glazed with warm apricot jam so they sit shiny and bright on the set custard. The custard must be cold and completely set before the berries go on, so this is an assembly-day dessert. It looks precise and takes less skill than it appears to.
sourceNotes: Fruit custard tarts belong to the French pâtisserie tradition, where tartes aux fruits are a patisserie case staple. The classic formulation (pâte sucrée case, crème pâtissière, fresh fruit, apricot glaze) is associated with the French boulangers-pâtissiers system and appears in Escoffier and in Larousse Gastronomique. The blueberry version is a summer adaptation of the strawberry and raspberry tarts more commonly associated with French patisseries.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry case can be blind baked 2 days ahead. The custard can be made 1 day ahead. Assemble on the day of serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A sweet shortcrust pastry made by the creaming method: butter and sugar are beaten together before the egg and flour are added. The result is smoother and more cake-like than a rubbed-in pastry, with a biscuit-like quality when baked. Pâte sucrée holds its shape precisely and is used for fruit tarts where the appearance of the case matters. It is less flaky than rough puff but easier to handle. The high fat and sugar content means it colours quickly — watch the temperature.

content.text: ; sweet and biscuit-like. It blind bakes to a pale gold. The custard filling must be completely cold and set before the blueberries are placed on top: warm custard will not hold the berries in place. The apricot glaze goes on last and sets quickly at room temperature. Serve on the day it is assembled.
Make and blind bake the pastry case

Beat 100 g softened butter with 50 g icing sugar until pale. Beat in 1 egg yolk and 0.25 tsp vanilla. Add 200 g flour and mix to a smooth dough. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Roll to 3 mm and line a 23 cm tart tin. Trim the overhang level. Refrigerate for 15 minutes. Heat the oven to 175 degrees C fan. Line with parchment and baking beans. Blind bake for 15 minutes. Remove beans and paper; bake for a further 8 to 10 minutes until dry and pale gold. Cool completely.

Heat 300 ml milk to just below simmering. Whisk 4 egg yolks with 80 g caster sugar, 30 g cornflour, and 1 tsp vanilla bean paste. Pour the hot milk over in a thin stream, whisking. Return to medium heat and stir continuously until thick and bubbling, about 3 minutes. Remove from the heat and stir in 20 g butter. Press cling film directly onto the surface and refrigerate until cold and firm; at least 2 hours.

Whisk the cold custard briefly to loosen it, then spread evenly into the cooled tart case. Refrigerate for 30 minutes until the surface is firmly set. Arrange 350 g blueberries across the surface. Brush the warm sieved apricot jam over the berries with a pastry brush. Leave at room temperature for 10 minutes for the glaze to set before slicing.

items.solution: The heat was too high or the mixture was not stirred continuously. Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly with a whisk or wooden spoon. If it lumps, strain through a fine-mesh sieve immediately and press out the solids before it cools.

items.problem: Pastry case shrank badly during blind baking
items.solution: The pastry was not chilled enough before baking, or the pastry was stretched rather than pressed into the tin. Always refrigerate the lined tin before baking, and ease rather than stretch the pastry around the edges.

items.problem: Berries rolled off when the tart was cut
items.solution: The glaze was not applied or not set, or the custard surface was not flat enough to hold the berries. Spread the custard level and refrigerate until firm before placing the berries. Glaze and allow 10 minutes to set before slicing.
